headline: Granada falls, Columbus sails, and the Atlantic becomes the hinge of a globalised world
summary: In January 1492 the last Muslim emirate on Iberian soil surrendered at Granada; in March the Catholic Monarchs ordered the expulsion of Spain's Jews; in October a Genoese captain in the pay of Castile made landfall in the Bahamas and set the Old and New Worlds on a collision course. In West Africa the empire of Songhai reached its territorial peak under Sunni Ali, who died in November; in Mesoamerica the Mexica under Ahuitzotl were consolidating a tributary empire from Tenochtitlan; in the Andes Huayna Capac was extending Inca rule into Ecuador; in Beijing the Hongzhi emperor governed a Ming realm turned inward from the sea; in Cairo and Istanbul the Mamluks and Ottomans were sliding toward the confrontation of the following generation.

snapshots.text: The year opened at Granada. On 2 January 1492 the emir Muhammad XII (Boabdil) surrendered the keys of the Alhambra to Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon, ending the nearly eight-century-old Muslim presence in Iberia known as al-Andalus. Boabdil rode into exile in the Alpujarras; Christian troops entered the palace complex without a fight. On 31 March, at the Alhambra itself, the Catholic Monarchs signed the Alhambra Decree ordering all Jews to convert or leave Castile and Aragon by the end of July; between forty thousand and two hundred thousand Sephardim departed by 2 August, many carried in Ottoman ships dispatched by Sultan Bayezid II to Salonica, Istanbul, and Izmir. On 17 April the same monarchs, at nearby Santa Fe, signed the Capitulations with the Genoese navigator Cristóbal Colón; on 3 August he sailed from Palos de la Frontera with the Santa María, Pinta, and Niña, and on 12 October made landfall on Guanahani in the Bahamas, which he named San Salvador. Elsewhere in Europe, Pope Innocent VIII died on 25 July and was succeeded on 11 August by the Valencian Rodrigo Borgia as Alexander VI. Casimir IV Jagiellon of Poland-Lithuania died on 7 June. On 3 November Henry VII of England and Charles VIII of France signed the Peace of Étaples ending Henry's Breton war. In Florence Lorenzo the Magnificent died on 8 April, opening the way to the friar Savonarola's Republic; in the same year Antonio da Sangallo the Elder and Bramante were reshaping Italian architecture, and Leonardo was in Milan.

Columbus's landfall on 12 October 1492 encountered the Lucayan Taíno inhabitants of Guanahani; over the following weeks the Santa María, Pinta, and Niña touched at Cuba, on 28 October, and at the northern coast of Hispaniola (which he named La Isla Española), where the Santa María ran aground on Christmas Eve and its timbers were used to build the fort of La Navidad, the first European settlement in the Americas. He returned to Spain with captive Taíno people and modest amounts of gold, arriving at Palos on 15 March 1493 and initiating a Columbian exchange of peoples, plants, animals, pathogens, and metals that would remake global demography. Beyond the Caribbean rim the great Native civilisations were oblivious to the encounter. In the Basin of Mexico the Mexica under Ahuitzotl (r. 1486-1502) ruled a growing tributary empire from Tenochtitlan on Lake Texcoco; the Templo Mayor had been rededicated in 1487 with mass sacrifice, and the emperor was campaigning southeastward into Oaxaca and the Soconusco. In the Yucatán the Maya League of Mayapan had recently collapsed (around 1441) into competing petty kingdoms. In the Andes, Huayna Capac had recently succeeded Túpac Inca Yupanqui (d. 1493) and was consolidating Tawantinsuyu, the Inca 'Realm of Four Parts', from Cuzco, extending campaigns northward into modern Ecuador with the founding of a second capital at Tumipampa (Cuenca). In North America, Mississippian polities were in decline while Ancestral Pueblo, Iroquoian, and countless other societies continued their independent trajectories.

In the western Sahel the Songhai empire had reached its territorial peak under Sunni Ali Ber (Sonni Ali), who had reigned from Gao since 1464. He had taken Timbuktu from the Tuareg in 1468 and, after an epic seven-year siege, the trading city of Djenne in 1473, bringing under his rule the great cities and trans-Saharan trade routes that had once belonged to Mali. He died on 6 November 1492 while returning from a campaign against the Fulani; he was succeeded briefly by his son Sonni Baru, who would be overthrown on 3 March 1493 by the general Muhammad Ture, who took the throne as Askia Muhammad I and inaugurated the Askia dynasty of orthodox Muslim reformers who would patronise Timbuktu's Sankore and Djinguereber mosques as centres of learning. In the central Sudan the Kanem-Bornu state under the Sayfawa mai continued a long recovery. In the Ethiopian highlands the Christian Solomonic emperor Eskender was reigning (from 1478) as Muslim sultanates in Adal and Ifat pressed the eastern frontier. In North Africa, the Zayyanids at Tlemcen, Wattasids at Fez (having supplanted the Marinids in 1472), and Hafsids at Tunis governed weakened states as Portuguese and Spanish crusading pressure on the Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts intensified; the Portuguese had established forts along the Gulf of Guinea and were about to reach the Cape. In East Africa the Swahili city-states of Kilwa, Mombasa, and Malindi were about to be shattered by the arrival of Vasco da Gama in 1498, and in the interior Great Zimbabwe's monumental culture had passed its peak.

The Islamic Mediterranean in 1492 was dominated by the rival Ottoman and Mamluk sultanates. In Istanbul, Sultan Bayezid II (r. 1481-1512), the scholarly and cautious son of Mehmed the Conqueror, presided over a state consolidating its recent conquests and rebuilding from the internal war with his brother Cem Sultan (then in Papal custody in Rome). In one of the year's most consequential decisions Bayezid dispatched Kemal Reis and the Ottoman navy to lift Sephardic and Muslim refugees from Iberian ports; tens of thousands of Sephardic Jews settled at Salonica (which soon became a Jewish-majority city), Istanbul, Izmir, Safed, and Sarajevo, transforming Ottoman urban commerce and printing. In Cairo the Mamluk sultan Qaitbay (r. 1468-1496), one of the last effective Mamluk rulers, patronised architectural masterpieces (the funerary complex outside Cairo bearing his name) while facing chronic fiscal stress and Portuguese pressure on the Red Sea spice trade. In Safavid Persia's territory the Aq Qoyunlu Turkoman federation still ruled, but the young Isma'il, future Shah Isma'il I and founder of the Safavid empire (from 1501), was already in hiding in Gilan. On the Arabian peninsula the Sharifate of Mecca, under Sharif Barakat II, remained an object of both Ottoman and Mamluk attention. Meanwhile in Al-Andalus the Muslim community — some five hundred thousand strong at the surrender — was subjected within a decade to forced conversion, giving rise to the Moriscos who would be expelled in 1609.

In China the Ming emperor Hongzhi (Zhu Youtang, r. 1487-1505), one of the most admired rulers of the dynasty, presided over what later historians called a Confucian minor renaissance from the Forbidden City in Beijing (established as capital in 1421). He purged the corrupt Buddhist and Daoist favourites of his predecessor's court, revived the grand secretariat, tightened the fiscal administration, and rebuilt sections of the northern Great Wall against Oirat pressure; his single-empress reign (no concubines) was remembered as an anomaly of imperial virtue. The Ming maritime bans of the previous century, imposed after Zheng He's early-fifteenth-century Indian Ocean voyages, remained in force, and the government's back was firmly turned to the sea just as European ships were beginning to reach it. In the countryside, the silver-based Single Whip tax reform was decades away but the commercial economy of the lower Yangtze — silk, cotton, porcelain — was flourishing. In Japan the Ashikaga shogunate under Ashikaga Yoshihisa's cousin Yoshitane was in the fractious post-Ōnin (1467-1477) civil war world of the Warring States (Sengoku) period; central authority had all but dissolved and daimyō warlords fought across the countryside. The Ryūkyū kingdom on Okinawa flourished as a trading intermediary between Ming China, Japan, and Southeast Asia. In Korea the Joseon dynasty under King Seongjong had recently died (December 1494 date is close but by 1492 he was still reigning), and the printed publication of the Gyeongguk daejeon (Grand Code) had consolidated Confucian statecraft.

North India in 1492 was politically fragmented. In Delhi the Lodi sultan Bahlul Khan had died in 1489 and his son Sikandar Lodi (r. 1489-1517) was consolidating the Afghan sultanate, moving the capital in 1504 to a newly founded Agra. The Sharqi kingdom of Jaunpur, absorbed in 1479, had been the sultanate's major eastern rival. In central India the Malwa sultanate at Mandu was flourishing under Ghiyas Shah's late reign, and Rajput states — Mewar under Rana Kumbha's successors and shortly Rana Sanga, Marwar, Amber — asserted regional autonomy. In the western Deccan the Bahmani sultanate was disintegrating into the five sultanates of Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, Berar, and Bidar, formalised over the following decade. To the south the Vijayanagara empire from its magnificent capital at Hampi on the Tungabhadra dominated the Karnataka-Telugu peninsula, its greatest king Krishnadevaraya still a child. In Bengal the Hussain Shahi dynasty had come to power in 1494 (Alauddin Husain Shah). In Kashmir the sultan Sikandar Shah's descendants ruled. In Timurid Central Asia, Sultan Husayn Bayqara's court at Herat, presided over by the polymath vizier Ali-Shir Nava'i and graced by the painter Bihzad, was in a cultural golden age; the young Babur, aged nine in 1492, would inherit Ferghana the following year. On the steppe the Uzbek confederation of Muhammad Shaybani was consolidating.

The Pacific in 1492 was oblivious to events across the Atlantic. The great Polynesian voyaging expansion had by this date reached and settled its outermost limits. In Aotearoa (New Zealand), Māori communities descended from voyagers of the late thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries had by now developed distinctive tribal (iwi) structures, kūmara horticulture, and elaborate pā fortifications; the moa had been hunted to extinction and settlement patterns were shifting from coastal to interior. On Rapa Nui (Easter Island) the ahu-and-moai monumental complex was probably at or near its peak, with hundreds of stone statues quarried at Rano Raraku raised on the ceremonial platforms of the coast. In the Hawaiian archipelago competing chiefdoms on each of the main islands supported dense wet-taro and dryland agriculture and complex ritual systems; the political consolidations that would produce the eighteenth-century Hawaiian kingdom lay ahead. Across western Polynesia the Tu'i Tonga maritime confederation continued its centuries-old dominance. In Australia hundreds of Aboriginal nations maintained their tens-of-thousands-of-years-old traditions of land management, ceremony, songline networks, and long-distance exchange in ochre, stone, pearl-shell, and other goods; specific dated events for individual communities in this year do not survive because these were oral societies. On the Papuan mainland horticultural societies farmed intensively in the highland valleys, while coastal Austronesian-speaking peoples sustained the exchange networks — such as the future Kula ring — that stretched across the Massim archipelagos.
